paper_title: $\mathrm{ECI}_{\mathrm{sem}}$: Semantic Residual Effective Contrastive Information for Evaluating Hard Negatives

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Hard-negative source selection for dense retrieval is usually decided only after fine-tuning and downstream evaluation. We propose $\mathrm{ECI}_{\mathrm{sem}}$, a semantic residual variant of Effective Contrastive Information (ECI) that ranks candidate negative sources using frozen target-encoder embeddings. $\mathrm{ECI}_{\mathrm{sem}}$ is training-free, not label-free: each scored example requires a query, a labeled positive, and an explicit candidate negative. $\mathrm{ECI}_{\mathrm{sem}}$ builds a weighted residual information matrix from target consistency, semantic locality, lexical residuality, and a log-determinant diversity objective. On MS MARCO negative sources, in-family $\mathrm{ECI}_{\mathrm{sem}}$ ranks LLM negatives highest among non-hybrid sources and Dense+LLM highest among hybrid sources, matching the strongest aggregate BEIR transfer results across DistilBERT, E5-base, and Contriever. Controlled ablations show that this alignment depends on using the target encoder family, while additional ablations show stability under sample-size, temperature, tokenizer, and IDF-corpus perturbations. The theory gives a local linearized link to loss reduction, while the empirical study treats downstream evaluation as the final test.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: ECIsem: Semantic Residual Effective Contrastive Information for Evaluating Hard Negatives Aarush Sinha1, Rahul Seetharaman2, Aman Bansal2 1University of Copenhagen, Department of Computer Science, Denmark 2Independent Researcher, USA 6 2 0 2 5 ] . [ 3 0 9 9 0 2 . 3 0 6 2 : r Abstract Hard-negative source selection for dense retrieval is usually decided only after fine-tuning and downstream evaluation. We propose ECIsem, semantic residual variant of Effective Contrastive Information (ECI) that ranks candidate negative sources using frozen target-encoder embeddings. ECIsem is training-free, not label-free: each scored example requires query, labeled positive, and an explicit candidate negative. ECIsem builds weighted residual information matrix from target consistency, semantic locality, lexical residuality, and log-determinant diversity objective. On MS MARCO negative sources, in-family ECIsem ranks LLM negatives highest among non-hybrid sources and Dense+LLM highest among hybrid sources, matching the strongest aggregate BEIR transfer results across DistilBERT, E5-base, and Contriever. Controlled ablations show that this alignment depends on using the target encoder family, while additional ablations show stability under sample-size, temperature, tokenizer, and IDFcorpus perturbations. The theory gives local linearized link to loss reduction, while the empirical study treats downstream evaluation as the final test. Introduction Dense retrieval systems are commonly trained with contrastive objectives that compare query with labeled positives and many negatives (Karpukhin et al. 2020; Xiong et al. 2020; Qu et al. 2021; Zhan et al. 2021; Robinson et al. 2021b). Hard negatives are especially important because they are close enough to the query to shape the representation. However, the best source of hard negatives is rarely known before training. Candidate sources include BM25 retrieval (Robertson and Zaragoza 2009), dense mining, reranking, diversityaware sampling (Moreira et al. 2024; Yang et al. 2024), LLM generation (Li et al. 2024; Sinha 2025), and structured evidence (Sinha et al. 2025). In practice, researchers often compare these sources by training separate models and evaluating them on retrieval benchmarks, which is expensive and gives little diagnostic insight into why one source helps more than another. The central difficulty is that hardness is not the same as usefulness. negative with high lexical overlap, high dense similarity, or high contrastive loss may be informative, but it may also be false negative, near-duplicate of the positive, an artifact of query-term matching, or redundant example of pattern already represented in the batch (Bonifacio et al. 2022; Chuang et al. 2020). This problem is sharper for synthetic negatives, where generated passages can look plausible while violating relevance assumptions. useful pre-training diagnostic should therefore ask not only whether negative is hard, but whether it is target consistent, semantically local, lexically nontrivial, and diverse across the source. We introduce ECIsem, semantic residual Effective Contrastive Information diagnostic for ranking hard-negative sources before fine-tuning. ECIsem uses frozen copy of the target encoder, so scores are computed in the same representation geometry that will initialize training. For each querypositive-negative triplet, ECIsem measures whether the encoder still prefers the positive over the negative, whether the negative is closer to the positive than to the query, and whether query-token overlap explains the match. These gates weight semantic residual directions, and log-determinant objective rewards sources that cover many non-redundant contrastive directions. ECIsem is intended as screening criterion, not as replacement for downstream evaluation. It is training-free because it does not require optimization, trained checkpoints, or benchmark labels at scoring time. It is not label-free: each scored record must contain query, at least one labeled positive passage, and explicit candidate negatives. The theoretical analysis connects ECIsem to expected loss reduction only under local linearization assumptions, and the empirical sections test whether the diagnostic is useful in practice. Contributions: We define ECIsem, log-determinant diagnostic that combines target consistency, semantic locality, lexical residuality, and residual diversity under frozen targetencoder geometry. We evaluate BM25, dense, LLM, and hybrid negative sources for MS MARCO fine-tuning, showing that infamily ECIsem rankings align with aggregate BEIR transfer across three retrieval backbones. We compare ECIsem against hardness and loss proxies, analyze sample-size and temperature stability, and test tokenizer, IDF-corpus, and explicit η/ψ failure-case sensitivity.

sections.title: Methodology
sections.content: Recent surveys organize dense-retrieval negative sampling into random, statically mined, dynamically mined, and synthetic sources (Wischounig, Abdallah, and Jatowt 2026). Dual-encoder retrieval, beginning with systems such as DPR (Karpukhin et al. 2020), relies on contrastive supervision and large negative sets (Gao, Yao, and Chen 2021; Xiong et al. 2021). Pretraining and weak-supervision methods such as Condenser, Contriever, GTR, and E5 show that retrieval quality depends on contrastive signal structure and stability (Gao and Callan 2021; Izacard et al. 2022; Ni et al. 2022; Wang et al. 2024). Negative selection is therefore central. BM25 and dense mining define different notions of hardness (Robertson and Zaragoza 2009; Zhan et al. 2021), while dynamic hardnegative training methods such as ANCE, STAR, and ADORE improve retrieval by refreshing or reweighting difficult examples (Xiong et al. 2021; Zhan et al. 2021). Balanced and structured sampling methods, including TAS-B, TriSampler, and SimANS, seek negatives that are informative without becoming likely false negatives (Hofstätter et al. 2021; Yang et al. 2024; Zhou et al. 2022). False negatives bias contrastive gradients (Robinson et al. 2021a; Chuang et al. 2020), and recent dense-retrieval work explicitly regularizes or filters these cases (Wang, Zhang, and Nguyen 2023). Thus hardness alone is an incomplete proxy for training value. LLMs provide another source of synthetic negatives. InPars, GPL, and Promptagator synthesize retrieval supervision (Bonifacio et al. 2022; Wang et al. 2022; Dai et al. 2023), while SyNeg and prompt-based generation construct hard negatives directly (Li et al. 2024; Sinha 2025). Such negatives can be useful but may violate relevance assumptions without filtering. Our log-determinant objective is also related to determinant-based diversity modeling, especially determinantal point processes (Kulesza and Taskar 2012), but ECIsem uses the determinant as training-free diagnostic over weighted residual directions rather than as probabilistic subset sampler. Prior work has connected log-determinant objectives to information gain, posterior uncertainty reduction, and experimental design. In Bayesian experimental design, D-optimal criteria maximize the log-determinant of an information matrix, corresponding to selecting observations that most reduce posterior uncertainty (Chaloner and Verdinelli 1995; Pukelsheim 2006). Related ideas have been used for active data selection and information-theoretic dataset valuation (MacKay 1992; Foster et al. 2019). Log-determinant objectives also discourage redundant observations through diminishing returns and have strong connections to submodular information-collection criteria (Krause and Golovin 2014). Similar covariance-volume objectives appear in selfsupervised learning to encourage diverse representation directions and prevent collapse (Bardes, Ponce, and LeCun 2022). Our work adapts these ideas to training-free hardnegative source evaluation by measuring the diversity of validity-weighted residual contrastive directions. Hard Negative Mining All mining methods use approximately 400,000 querypassage pairs from MS MARCO (Bajaj et al. 2018) via Tevatron/msmarco-passage1. BM25 We index the corpus with BM25S(Lù 2024) and keep the top = 10 non-positive passages per query. Dense Retriever Mining We construct dense hard negatives using msmarco-MiniLM-L6-v3. Queries and passages are encoded with frozen normalized embeddings. Labeled positives are removed, and the top = 10 remaining passages by cosine similarity are retained: Formally, for query embedding eq and passage embedding ed, and s(q, d) = cos(eq, ed), (1) Ndense(q) = TopKdCP(q) s(q, d), where is the passage corpus and P(q) is the set of labeled positives. (2) LLM We use Qwen3-30B-A3B-Thinking-2507 (yan 2025) with vLLM (Kwon et al. 2023) on two NVIDIA L40s GPUs. Given query and positive document, the prompt asks for ten JSON-formatted negatives that share topical cues, appear initially relevant, and then omit or contradict the true information need while remaining plausible and diverse. The full prompt template appears in the appendix. ECIsem: Semantic Residual Effective Contrastive Information We compute ECIsem with frozen copy of the target encoder, matching downstream query/document formatting, maximum sequence length, embedding normalization, and temperature. Let be candidate file. Each record contains query qt, labeled positive passage pt, and deduplicated hard-negative set Nt = {nt,1, . . . , nt,Kt}. (3) When multiple positives are present, we use the first labeled positive. All explicit negatives are scored, so files with different Kt are compared per negative rather than by raw file size. We assume ND = (cid:80) Kt > 0 and exclude files with no explicit negatives. Thus ECIsem is suitable for supervised or weakly supervised negative-source selection, not for fully unlabeled corpora with no relevance information. Let f0 be the frozen target encoder. We apply the same formatting used in training, including query: and passage: prefixes for E5 models. The unit-normalized embeddings are ut = f0(qt), v+ = f0(pt), t,k = f0(nt,k), (4) ut2 = v+ 2 = t,k2 = 1. 1https://huggingface.co/datasets/Tevatron/msmarco-passage For each triplet (qt, pt, nt,k), we define three scalar gates. The pairwise target-consistency gate is τ ρt,k = σ (cid:32) v+ v t,k (cid:33) , (5) where τ = 0.05 matches the default MNRL scale 20 = 1/τ . The relative semantic-locality gate is ηt,k = σ (cid:32) (v+ )v t,k t,k τ (cid:33) . (6) To reduce lexical shortcuts, let (x) be normalized alphanumeric tokens and let idf(a) be nonnegative smoothed IDF weight computed over fixed candidate scoring corpus. The lexical coverage and residual factors are We Fine-Tuning Non-hybrid runs use BM25, Dense, and Qwen3-30B files with ten negatives per record, yielding 4M flattened triplets from 400k MS MARCO queries. Hybrid runs use BM25+Dense, BM25+LLM, and Dense+LLM files with = 20, yielding 8M triplets per source. with CachedMultipleNegativesRankingLoss (Reimers and Gurevych 2019), GradCache MNRL implementation (Gao et al. 2021). We use query_to_doc, BatchSamplers.NO_DUPLICATES, batch size = 4096, and GradCache mini-batches Benc = min(2048, B). i=1, the query_to_doc MNRL denominator pools all positives and explicit negatives: For batch of flattened triplets {(qi, pi, ni)}B train C(qt, nt,k) = (cid:80) aT (qt)T (nt,k) idf(a) (cid:80) aT (qt) idf(a) , (7) LMNRL = 1 (cid:88) log (cid:80) i=1 j=1 {nj}B dDB j=1. DB = {pj}B exp(s(qi, pi)/τ ) exp(s(qi, d)/τ ) (13) ψt,k = 1 C(qt, nt,k). (8) If the denominator is zero, we set C(qt, nt,k) = 0, so ψt,k = 1. rt,k = The semantic residual direction is v+ t,k v+ t,k2 with the convention rt,k = 0 when v+ = residual information matrix is Kt(cid:88) (cid:88) , (cid:98)I sem = 1 ND k=1 (9) t,k. The semantic ρt,kηt,kψt,krt,kr t,k, (10) where ND = (cid:80) The score is Kt is the number of explicit negatives. ECIsem(D) = log det (cid:16) Id + (cid:98)I sem (cid:17) , (11) We also report ECIsem(D)/d, (12) where is the embedding dimension. Higher scores indicate broader non-redundant target-consistent, semantically local, lexically residual directions under fixed per-negative sampling budget. The three gates are intended to separate usefulness from raw hardness. ρt,k downweights negatives that the frozen target encoder already ranks above the labeled positive, since these cases may reflect false negatives, annotation noise, or target-model uncertainty. ηt,k favors negatives that remain near the positive passage in document space, rather than examples that are merely close to the query by lexical or topical shortcut. ψt,k discounts negatives whose apparent difficulty is largely explained by query-token overlap. The log-determinant then asks whether the remaining weighted residual directions add new contrastive coverage instead of repeating the same source-specific pattern. For interpretability, we report mean target-consistency ρt,k, semantic-locality ηt,k, lexical coverage C(qt, nt,k), residual weight ψt,k, pairwise loss log ρt,k, and inversion rate 1[ρt,k < 0.5]. These are diagnostics only; the ranking score is Eq. (11). The appendix gives the theoretical framework. We use τ = 0.05, equivalently logit scale of 1/τ = 20, matching the ECIsem temperature. DistilBERT (Sanh et al. 2020), E5-base (Wang et al. 2024), and Contriever (Izacard et al. 2022) are trained for one epoch with maximum sequence length 128, learning rate 2 105, and warmup ratio 0.1. Mixed precision uses bfloat16 or fp16 via AMP when available. Following standard practice in dense retrieval studies (Moreira et al. 2024; Wang et al. 2022; Karpukhin et al. 2020; Sinha et al. 2025), we report single-seed results. Computational Cost In logged Contriever run, scoring all six sources with ECIsem on one RTX6000 Pro took 8.5 GPU-hours wallclock, including 8.3 GPU-hours for scoring. The corresponding downstream pipeline took 17.9 GPU-hours for fine-tuning and 38.9 for BEIR evaluation. ECIsem therefore provided the source ranking signal at roughly 15% of the measured downstream compute budget. Results Table 1 reports in-family ECIsem scores computed with the same backbone used for fine-tuning. This in-family setting is important because ECIsem evaluates source quality in the geometry that will later receive the contrastive update. Across DistilBERT, E5-base, and Contriever, LLM negatives receive the highest non-hybrid scores. The pattern indicates that the generated negatives contain valid contrastive directions that are less lexically explained and less redundant than the BM25 or dense-only alternatives. Among hybrid sources, Dense+LLM is consistently highest, suggesting complementarity between dense retrieval and generation: dense mining supplies local retrieved confusions, while generation adds broader residual directions. Table 2 reports BEIR (Thakur et al. 2021) zero-shot nDCG@10 after MS MARCO fine-tuning. Higher in-family ECIsem aligns with stronger aggregate transfer. LLM mining gives the best non-hybrid mean nDCG@10 for DistilBERT (0.392), E5-base (0.479), and Contriever (0.434). The Table 1: Training-free ECIsem scores. Bold marks the best score within each block. Model Setting Mining method ECIsem DistilBERT E5-base Contriever Non-hybrid Hybrid Non-hybrid Hybrid Non-hybrid Hybrid 0.1265 BM25 0.1755 Dense 0.2612 LLM BM25+Dense 0.1510 BM25+LLM 0.1939 Dense+LLM 0.2192 0.0954 BM25 0.1191 Dense 0.2112 LLM BM25+Dense 0.1072 BM25+LLM 0.1533 Dense+LLM 0.1659 0.0667 BM25 0.1170 Dense 0.1813 LLM BM25+Dense 0.0918 BM25+LLM 0.1240 Dense+LLM 0. ECIsem / dim. 0.000165 0.000228 0.000340 0.000197 0.000252 0.000285 0.000124 0.000155 0.000275 0.000140 0.000200 0.000216 0.000087 0.000152 0.000236 0.000120 0.000161 0.000195 highest-scoring hybrid source also has the best mean downstream performance for each backbone. The main claim is not that ECIsem predicts every dataset column, but that it provides useful training-free source ranking for aggregate transfer before expensive fine-tuning and BEIR evaluation. Training-Free Ablation Experiments We evaluate robustness to training-free design choices using 25,000 valid records per source, uniform sampling, and seed 42. Most ablations use frozen prdev/mini-gte embeddings2 with maximum sequence length 128, normalized embeddings, first-positive aggregation, and τ = 0.05 unless stated otherwise. No fine-tuning is performed. Baseline Diagnostics Table 3 compares ECIsem with simpler training-free diagnostics on the same 25,000 seeded records per source. This experiment addresses fairness concern in proxy comparisons: if ECIsem is computed with the target retriever but the baselines are computed with separate surrogate encoder, then differences could partly reflect encoder mismatch rather than the scoring criterion itself. We therefore compute every diagnostic with the same frozen encoder family that initializes the corresponding fine-tuning run. The local proxies measure different notions of hardness. Mean gt2 2 estimates local gradient energy, the MNRL proxy measures the two-document contrastive loss induced by the candidate negative, and ambiguity measures the rate at which the frozen encoder prefers the candidate negative over the labeled positive. These quantities are useful diagnostics, but they primarily reward difficult or inverted examples. In contrast, ECIsem weights residual directions by 2https://huggingface.co/prdev/mini-gte target consistency, semantic locality, and lexical residuality before applying the log-determinant diversity objective. Across DistilBERT, E5-base, and Contriever, ECIsem consistently ranks LLM highest among non-hybrid sources and Dense+LLM highest among hybrid sources. This matches the strongest mean BEIR transfer pattern in Table 2. The simpler proxies often select dense-heavy sources instead: MNRL and ambiguity favor Dense in every non-hybrid block and BM25+Dense in every hybrid block, while gradient energy is less consistent and also favors BM25-heavy or dense-heavy sources for E5-base and Contriever. This disagreement shows that the ECIsem ranking is not reducible to raw hardness, local contrastive loss, or inversion/ambiguity rates alone. Instead, the useful source-level signal appears to come from combining validity-weighted residual directions with nonredundant coverage. Simplification Stress Test We next ask whether the full ECIsem ranking can be recovered by simpler alternatives. Rather than reporting all singlegate removals, we focus on three stronger simplifications: gradient-weighted log-determinant score, margin-hardness log-determinant score based on local query-gradient geometry, and an unweighted residual-only log-determinant score. For each target backbone and setting, we report rank stability with respect to the full ECIsem ordering. Rank stability is the fraction of pairwise source orderings that agree with full ECIsem within the same three-source block; 1.0 indicates identical rank order and 0.0 indicates complete disagreement. Table 4 shows that the simpler alternatives do not consistently reproduce the full ECIsem ordering. Gradientweighted scoring matches DistilBERT but is unstable for E5-base and Contriever, while margin-hardness is the least stable across backbones. Residual-only diversity is more stable in some hybrid blocks, but it removes the validity, locality, and lexical-residual weighting that distinguish ECIsem from unweighted residual coverage. These results indicate that the full diagnostic is not equivalent to raw hardness or unweighted residual diversity. Sample-Size Stability ECIsem rankings are unchanged at 25%, 50%, 75%, and 100% of each seeded sample. LLM remains highest among non-hybrid sources, and Dense+LLM remains highest among hybrid sources. The stability value of 1.000 denotes exact agreement with the full-sample rank order. This does not prove convergence for arbitrary datasets, but it indicates that the observed ordering is not an artifact of using the maximum available sample size in this experiment. Tokenizer, IDF, and Failure-Case Sensitivity We test the implementation-sensitive gates η and ψ on 5,000 seeded rows per source. The default lexical setting uses alphanumeric tokens and pooled-document IDF. Tokenizer variants are alphanumeric, alphanumeric length at least two, whitespace, and character trigrams. IDF variants are pooled documents, source-specific documents, pooled query-document text, and uniform weights. Table 2: BEIR nDCG@10 after MS MARCO fine-tuning. Results are grouped by backbone and by non-hybrid versus hybrid mining. Mean averages the listed datasets. Bold marks the best score within each block and column. Setting Source ρ < 0.5 η 0.25 0.50 Valid High-C Valid Low-η Non-hybrid BM25 Dense LLM Hybrid BM25+Dense BM25+LLM Dense+LLM 16.8 33.5 2.1 25.2 9.5 17.7 43.5 41.4 39.6 42.4 41.6 40.5 89.6 69.3 38.0 79.5 63.8 53. 7.9 4.6 4.1 6.2 6.0 4.3 < 0.1 2.9 2.6 1.5 1.3 2.8 Table 6 shows modest score shifts but stable rankings: LLM > Dense > BM25 for non-hybrid sources, and Dense+LLM > BM25+LLM > BM25+Dense for hybrid sources. The largest tokenizer effects occur when changing the granularity of lexical matching, especially for dense and LLM-containing sources. IDF-corpus changes have smaller effects for LLM and Dense+LLM than for BM25-heavy sources. This pattern is consistent with the role of ψ: BM25 negatives often share many query terms, so their residual weight depends more strongly on the lexical coverage estimate. LLM negatives have lower mean coverage and higher mean ψ, making their ECIsem scores less tied to the exact IDF corpus. Table 7 reports failure buckets. We use ρ < 0.5 for inversions, η 0.25 for low locality, and 0.50 for high lexical coverage. Valid High-C denotes ρ 0.75, η 0.75, and 0.50; Valid Low-η denotes ρ 0.75, ψ 0.75, and η 0.25. BM25-heavy sources have the highest high-coverage rates, so ψ removes much lexical shortcut signal. Non-hybrid LLM negatives have lower inversion and coverage rates, consistent with their higher ECIsem. The valid high-coverage and valid low-η buckets expose cases where gates may suppress plausible negatives, making failure modes auditable. The buckets Figure 1: Temperature sweep for lexical-debiased pairwise ECIsem. Rank order is stable within each setting. also separate distinct failure modes. An inversion indicates that the frozen target encoder already prefers the candidate negative over the labeled positive, which can signal label noise, false negative, or model-specific weakness. Low η indicates that the negative is not close to the positive relative to the query, so it may be too easy or topically displaced. High lexical coverage indicates that the example may be solvable by query-term overlap alone. These categories do not prove that an individual negative is invalid, but they provide measurable audit groups for inspecting when η and ψ help and when they may be overly conservative. Temperature Sweep Figure 1 evaluates τ {0.03, 0.05, 0.1}. Temperature changes absolute scale but not the within-setting rank order, supporting τ = 0.05 as stable default. Smaller τ sharpens the gates and gives more weight to confident pairwise differences; larger τ smooths those differences. The unchanged ordering shows that the source ranking is not driven by narrow temperature choice. Scope and Limitations ECIsem should be interpreted as source-screening diagnostic for supervised or weakly supervised hard-negative construction. It requires query-positive supervision and explicit candidate negatives, so it cannot score fully unlabeled corpus without an additional retrieval or annotation step. The method also scores negatives under frozen encoder. This is desirable when the goal is to choose source before training, but it means that poorly calibrated frozen encoder can misestimate target consistency or locality. The theoretical connection to training is local. The lossreduction argument uses first-order expansion around the initialization and treats frozen embedding residuals as proxy for parameter-space gradient structure. It therefore explains why diverse valid residual directions can be useful, but it does not guarantee global optimization improvement or generalization for every dataset. This is why we pair the theory with downstream BEIR evaluation and with failure buckets that expose cases where the gates may be too strict or too permissive. Conclusion We introduced ECIsem, training-free semantic residual diagnostic for screening hard-negative sources with frozen encoder embeddings. Across BM25, dense, LLM, and hybrid negatives, in-family ECIsem aligns with aggregate downstream transfer, while controlled mini-gte ablations show that this alignment depends on encoder choice rather than on hardness alone. Additional ablations show stable rankings under sample-size, temperature, tokenizer, and IDF-corpus perturbations, and make η/ψ failure modes explicit. ECIsem is practical screening criterion, not substitute for downstream evaluation, mutual-information guarantee, or label-free method. It requires query-positive supervision and explicit candidate negatives. Future work should extend it to adaptive data selection, multilingual retrieval, unlabeled candidate discovery, and diagnostics for diversity, factuality, and human relevance.

Structural Analysis and Motivation This section provides structural analysis of ECIsem. Rather than offering strict optimization or distribution-free generalization guarantees, our goal is to motivate the design choices of the diagnostic and analyze its mathematical properties. ECIsem is target-aligned diagnostic, not an exact full-batch MNRL gradient estimator. It uses the target encoder, formatting, normalization, and temperature to measure weighted residual volume from explicit negatives. Target Contrastive Setting Each hard-negative file is flattened into triplets (qi, pi, ni), where qi is query, pi is its labeled positive passage, and ni is one explicit negative. row with negatives contributes triplets, giving per-negative comparisons across non-hybrid and hybrid sources. Let a() and b() be the deterministic target formatting maps for queries and passages, including role prefixes such as query: and passage: when required by the encoder family. With frozen, unit-normalized target-encoder embeddings, ui = f0(a(qi)), v+ = f0(b(pi)), = f0(b(ni)), where ui2 = v+ For target MNRL batch 2 = 2 = 1. = {(qi, pi, ni) : = 1, . . . , b}, define two document pools: D0 = {p1, . . . , pb}, is the positive-only baseline and D1 D1 = {p1, . . . , pb, n1, . . . , nb}. adds explicit negD0 atives. For {0, 1} and Ds B, define zi,d = vd τ , (d) = πs (cid:80) exp(zi,d) exp(zi,c) cDs . The corresponding per-query target loss is = log πs ℓs (pi), = (cid:88) i= ℓs . (14) Fine-tuning uses the full in-batch softmax over D1 B; ECI uses scalable pairwise restriction of the same target geometry. Pairwise Target-Consistency Gate Exact marginal changes from 0 require full inbatch interactions. For scalable source screening, we use the frozen target encoders two-document judgment between the labeled positive and each explicit negative. to 1 For flattened triplet (q, p, n), the two-document softmax probability assigned to the labeled positive is Pτ (p q) = exp(uv+/τ ) exp(uv+/τ ) + exp(uv/τ ) (cid:18) uv+ uv τ (cid:19) = σ (15) = ρ(q, p, n). Thus log ρ(q, p, n) is exactly the two-document restriction of the MNRL loss for the positive and the candidate negative. ρ is target-consistency gate, not gradient-magnitude proxy. It is large when the frozen retriever assigns higher probability to the labeled positive than to the candidate negative, reducing weight on inverted or potentially mislabeled negatives. The semantic-neighborhood factor is η(q, p, n) = σ (cid:18) (v+)v uv τ (cid:19) . (16) This heuristic favors negatives closer to the labeled positive passage than to the query embedding under the frozen representation. It is relative locality gate, not an absolute nearest-neighbor claim. To reduce sparse-retrieval shortcuts, set ψ(q, n) = 1 C(q, n), where (x) is the set of normalized alphanumeric tokens. We compute nonnegative smoothed IDF weights over fixed candidate scoring corpus, for example idf(a) = log + 1 df(a) + + 1, where is the number of texts in the candidate scoring corpus and df(a) is the number of such texts containing token a. sections.title:
Semantic Residual Information
The residual direction associated with a triplet is
sections.content: r(q, p, n) = v+ v+ v2 . If v+ = v, set r(q, p, n) = 0. For candidate file D, the semantic residual information matrix is (cid:98)I sem = 1 ND (cid:88) (q,p,n)D w(q, p, n) r(q, p, n)r(q, p, n), (19) where ND is the number of explicit negatives in the flattened candidate file. The final diagnostic is ECIsem(D) = log det (cid:16) Id + (cid:98)I sem (cid:17) . (20) The log-determinant rewards non-redundant residual volume rather than raw hardness. Proposition 1 (Structural properties of ECIsem). For fixed frozen encoder, query/document formatting, temperature, tokenization rule, IDF corpus, and candidate file, assume that embeddings are unit-normalized, IDF weights are nonnegative, ND > 0, and residual directions use the convention r(q, p, n) = 0 when v+ = v. Then: 1. (cid:98)I sem 2. ECIsem(D) 0. 3. The factor ρ(q, p, n) is exactly the positive probability under the two-document restriction of the same temperaturescaled query-document softmax used by MNRL. is positive semidefinite. 4. For fixed total residual weight, the log-determinant objective assigns strictly larger value when positive weight is spread across at least two non-collinear residual directions than when the same total weight is concentrated in single unit direction. More generally, additional linearly independent weighted residual directions contribute positive higher-order volume terms. Thus ρ is the positive probability under the two-document temperature-scaled target softmax. For redundancy sensitivity, let r1, . . . , rm be unit residual directions with positive weights a1, . . . , am and = (cid:80)m j=1 aj. If all weight is concentrated in one unit direction r, the only nonzero eigenvalue is , so log det(Id + rr) = log(1 + ). least If at two positive-weighted directions are noncollinear, let = [r1, . . . , rm] and = diag(a1, . . . , am). The matrix determinant lemma gives det(Id + RAR) = det(Im + A1/2RRA1/2). Let = RR and = A1/2GA1/2. Then is positive semidefinite, Bjj = aj, and tr(B) = (cid:88) j=1 aj = W. The determinant expansion of Im + in principal minors gives det(Im + B) = 1 + tr(B) + (cid:88) det(BS), S{1,...,m} S2 where BS is the principal submatrix indexed by S. All principal minors are nonnegative. Non-collinearity gives subset = {j, k} such that det(BS) = ajak (cid:0)1 (r rk)2(cid:1) > 0. Consequently, det(Id + RAR) = det(Im + B) > 1 + W, and hence log det(Id + RAR) > log(1 + ). Thus spreading the same total weight across non-collinear directions yields larger log-determinant than concentrating it in one direction. Higher-order Gram principal minors add nonnegative volume terms and are positive for linearly independent weighted directions. Connection to Loss Reduction and Information Volume We next relate the matrix form to contrastive optimization to motivate our design. The connection is local: exact for the two-document loss at frozen embeddings, and tied to parameter updates only through encoder linearization. For one triplet, define the two-document loss ℓ2(q, p, n) = log ρ(q, p, n) (cid:18) (cid:18) = log 1 + exp u(v+ v) τ (cid:19)(cid:19) . (22) Let δ(q, p, n) = v+ and let = δ/δ2 when δ = 0. Proposition 2 (Pairwise gradient information). Treat the normalized query embedding as an ambient Euclidean variable and hold v+ and fixed. If δ = v+ = 0, then uℓ2(q, p, n) = 1 ρ(q, p, n) τ δ(q, p, n), (23) and therefore uℓ2(q, p, n)uℓ2(q, p, n) = (1 ρ(q, p, n))2 τ 2 r(q, p, n)r(q, p, n). v+ v2 2 (24) Proof. Let = uδ τ . Then ρ(q, p, n) = σ() and ℓ2 = log(1 + exp()). Since ℓ2 = (1 σ()) = (1 ρ(q, p, n)), and = δ τ , Eq. (23) follows by the chain rule. Taking the outer product and using δ = δ2r gives Eq. (24). Proposition 2 exposes fundamental design choice. Raw gradient magnitude is proportional to (1 ρ)2, which inherently favors low ρ (i.e., hard negatives). However, small ρ can also indicate inverted or mislabeled negatives with large but unreliable gradients. Crucially, ECIsem is intentionally not gradientenergy estimator; it trades raw hardness for targetconsistent validity. By using the weight ρ η ψ instead of the pure gradient-energy weight (1ρ)2 2ηψ, ρ acts as target-consistency gate. Thus, ECIsem is validityweighted analogue of the gradient information matrix. (Note: The empirical section directly tests this tradeoff by comparing validity-weighted scoring against raw hardness weighting.) τ 2 v+ For one-step loss-reduction statement, let L(θ) be differentiable with β-Lipschitz gradient, and let gD be sourceinduced stochastic update at initialization. Smoothness gives E[L(θ)L(θαgD)] α θL(θ), E[gD] E[gD2 2]. (25) Without an alignment assumption, no pre-training scalar score can guarantee global loss reduction. Our gates simply favor examples more likely to align with valid supervision. βα2 2 Information-Volume Intuition (Motivation). In highly idealized, local linearized Gaussian model with isotropic prior precision λ > 0, the embedding-space posterior covariance associated with fixed per-negative source-sampling budget takes the form: (cid:16) Σemb = λId + (cid:98)I sem specific interpretation, (cid:17)1 . (26) the negative logthis Under determinant of the covariance is log det Σemb = log det (cid:16) λId + (cid:98)I sem (cid:17) , (27) log-determinant mirrors meaning the ECI the local information-volume term when λ = 1. This provides an intuitive motivation for ECI through reduced uncertainty volume, but it is not distribution-free generalization guarantee. rigorous parameter-space bound would require strong, often unrealistic assumptions on encoder Jacobians, document-side gradients, label noise, and the optimization trajectory, which are beyond the scope of this structural analysis. LLM Prompt to Generate Hard-Negatives Prompt 1 Assume you are an expert in {domain_name}, and there is example 2 with "user_query" and its related doc 3 "positive_document". 4 example: {positive_example} 5 6 [Task Definition] 7 Your task is to write {num_hard_negatives} hard negative 8 samples in JSON format. The JSON object 9 must contain the following keys: 10 11 - "reasoning": string, reasoning steps 12 on how to generate {num_hard_negatives} hard negative documents. 13 14 15 {key_bullets} 16 17 [Reasoning Definition] 18 19 - Write the inference process step 20 21 22 by step in "reasoning", including how to associate from the "user_query" and "positive_document" to get the hard-negative documents. 23 24 [Hard Negatives Definition] 25 26 - All the hard negative documents should use similar keywords or topics as the "positive_document". 28 29 30 - All the hard negative documents 31 33 34 appear to address the "user_query" at first glance. However, subtly diverges in content or context such that it does not truly answer the query or meet the users information need. 35 36 - All the hard negative documents should 37 38 be plausible and accurate documents; they should be diverse in topic, sources, and styles. 39 40 41 [Attributes Definition] 42 43 - All the negative documents should be in 44 the education level of {difficult_level} to comprehend, and the length should be {length} the "positive_document". 46 47 48 [Format Definition] 49 50 - Your output must always be JSON object only; do not explain yourself or output anything else.
